2024 · ACL 2025 · Benchmarks & Evals

MMMU-Pro: A More Robust Multi-discipline Multimodal Understanding Benchmark

MMMU had a leak: text-only LLMs could answer some of its "multimodal" questions without ever seeing the image, just by pattern-matching the options. MMMU-Pro plugs it — filter out text-solvable items, widen four options to ten, then embed the question itself inside a photo or screenshot so the model has to read and see at once. Every model tested falls further, some by more than 25 points.

Xiang Yue, Tianyu Zheng, Yuansheng Ni, Yubo Wang, Kai Zhang, Shengbang Tong, Yuxuan Sun, Botao Yu, Ge Zhang, Huan Sun, Yu Su, Wenhu Chen, Graham Neubig · MMMU Team (CMU / Ohio State / Waterloo)
venue ACL 2025 citations 488 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

MMMU launched in late 2023 as the college-exam benchmark for "Expert AGI" — 11.5K questions across six disciplines and 30 subjects, each pairing text with a genuinely heterogeneous image (charts, chemical structures, music scores, medical scans). It became the standard multimodal leaderboard almost overnight, cited on every frontier model card. GPT-4o reached 69.1% on it — a number treated as evidence of real progress in expert-level multimodal reasoning.

The MMMU-Pro authors went looking for the catch, and found two of them. First, text-only dependency: a meaningful slice of MMMU's questions don't actually need the image — the stem alone gives it away. Second, and more interesting, shortcut exploitation: even on questions that genuinely require the image for a human, strong text-only LLMs can often guess the right multiple-choice option anyway, leveraging pre-training priors and correlations between the candidate options rather than anything in the picture. Figure 2 shows Llama-3-70B-Instruct answering two MMMU questions correctly with zero visual input — one by simply knowing 19th-century US history trivia, the other by guessing the most "textbook-sounding" answer to a diagram it never saw.

Two more structural weaknesses compound this: MMMU's four-option multiple choice gives a 25% guessing floor, and separating text from images doesn't match how people actually use these models — screenshots and photos routinely mix both in a single image, which a four-option, always-typed-out-separately benchmark never tests. If a benchmark's headline number partly reflects memorized trivia and lucky guessing rather than seeing the figure, it's measuring something other than what it claims to measure — and every model card built on top of it inherits the inflation.

// how it works

The method

MMMU-Pro rebuilds MMMU through a three-step pipeline (Figure 1), each step targeting one of the leaks above.

1 · Filter out text-solvable questions

Four strong open text-only LLMs — Llama3-70B-Instruct, Qwen2-72B-Instruct, Yi-1.5-34B-Chat, and Mixtral-8×22B-Instruct — are forced to answer every MMMU question with no image, ten times each (even when they protest that they need the image). A question is deemed "answerable" by a model if it's correct on more than five of ten tries; any question where at least three of the four models answer it correctly across the majority of trials is dropped as text-solvable. From the surviving pool, 1,800 questions are sampled evenly across 30 subjects (60 each).

2 · Augment 4 options to 10

Even after filtering, some residual shortcut room remains in a 4-way multiple choice. GPT-4o generates six additional distractor options per question, Claude 3.5 Sonnet filters them for quality, and two rounds of human review refine and verify the result — a step that also catches and removes 70 more questions with weak image-question coherence, leaving 1,730 questions. Figure 3 in the paper shows this two-step squeeze in action: text-only accuracy on Llama-3-70B-Instruct falls from 41.4% (original MMMU items) to 34.2% (after filtering) to 20.3% (after 10-option augmentation) — and the same downward staircase holds for all four sieve models (Qwen2-72B: 40.7→33.5→18.9; Yi-1.5-34B: 37.7→30.7→17.1; Mixtral-8x22B: 34.3→29.0→16.7).

3 · The vision-only input setting

The sharpest design choice: for every one of the 1,730 questions, annotators also manually photograph or screenshot the question embedded in an image — varying background, font style and size to mimic how people actually screenshot content — so the model gets no separate text channel at all, only a picture containing both the prose and the figure. This yields a second, parallel 1,730-question set, for 3,460 questions total. The idea directly targets a human cognitive skill current MLLMs are not explicitly trained for: seeing and reading simultaneously rather than processing a pre-separated text stream and an image stream.

Models are scored under three settings — Standard (4 options), Standard (10 options), and Vision — each with both a direct-answer prompt and a chain-of-thought prompt (the higher of the two is reported). MMMU-Pro's official score is the average of the 10-option Standard result and the Vision result; the original 4-option Standard number is reported only as a bridge back to MMMU for comparison. Since a full from-scratch human study would be costly, the paper approximates human-expert performance by reusing MMMU's original human evaluation data (justified because the underlying question content is unchanged and MMMU's human evaluators had to show written work, which curbs guessing) — an approximation, not a fresh measurement, and the paper is explicit about that.

// what they found

The results

Every model the authors evaluated falls, and by a lot. GPT-4o (0513) drops from 69.1% on MMMU (Val) to 54.0% on 10-option Standard (∆1 = −15.1) and 49.7% on Vision (∆2 = −19.4). Claude 3.5 Sonnet falls from 68.3% to an MMMU-Pro overall of 51.5% — a 16.8-point decline. Gemini 1.5 Pro (0801) falls from 65.8% to 46.9% overall, an 18.9-point drop. The steepest fall belongs to an open model: VILA-1.5-40B collapses from 51.9% on MMMU (Val) to a 25.0% overall on MMMU-Pro — a 26.9-point loss, exactly the top of the "16.8% to 26.9%" range the abstract advertises.

Two separate effects stack to produce the total drop. Widening 4 options to 10 alone (∆1) costs GPT-4o (0513) 10.7 points (64.7% → 54.0%) — evidence that a real chunk of MMMU's original score was guessable from the option set. The vision-only setting then costs a further 4.3 points for GPT-4o (54.0% → 49.7%), and considerably more for weaker vision-language integrators: LLaVA-OneVision-72B loses 14.0 points (38.0% → 24.0%) purely from having to read the question off the image itself, and VILA-1.5-40B loses over 20 points on the same axis (35.9% → 14.1%).

Human-expert performance (approximated) barely moves across settings — 88.6% → 85.4% (Standard-10) → 85.4% (Vision) at the "high" tier — confirming these are genuine capability gaps for models, not artifacts that would trip up people too.

OCR isn't the bottleneck

A natural hypothesis is that vision-only performance is limited by simply reading the text out of the image. The authors test this directly: they measure each model's OCR accuracy (Levenshtein similarity between the model's transcription of the embedded question and the ground truth) and compare Vision-setting accuracy with vs. without an explicit "first transcribe, then answer" prompt.

ModelOCR acc.Vision w/ OCR promptVision w/o OCR prompt
GPT-4o92.349.749.4
Gemini 1.5 Pro (0801)89.744.443.6
InternVL2-Llama3-76B88.138.037.9
LLaVA-OneVision-72B87.824.023.8
MiniCPM-V2.667.024.221.1
LLaVA-NeXT-7B36.614.614.3

OCR accuracy spans a wide range across the 13 models tested (36.6% to 92.3%), with the strongest proprietary and open models clustered from the mid-80s up to GPT-4o's 92.3% — and for those higher-OCR models, an explicit OCR prompt barely moves final accuracy either way, meaning the vision-only drop for the leaders is not primarily a text-extraction failure. Tellingly, LLaVA-OneVision-72B roughly matches GPT-4o mini and InternVL2-Llama3-76B on OCR accuracy (87.8% vs. 89.6%/88.1%) but trails both badly on final Vision accuracy (24.0% vs. 35.2%/38.0%) — reading the words is not the same as reasoning over them once they're inside a picture.

Where models actually fail

Hand-annotating 60 GPT-4o error cases in the Vision setting attributes 46% to reasoning errors, 27% to perceptual errors, 25% to lack of knowledge, 2% to annotation errors, and effectively 0% to OCR errors.

That reasoning-error share is nearly double MMMU's original 26%, and the paper's qualitative pass names the recurring failure modes: models select the "closest" of ten similar-sounding options instead of committing to a definitive answer; simultaneously parsing text and image raises apparent cognitive load, so a model can correctly extract all the text from an image and still fail to answer the question; visually similar or overlapping chart elements can distract a model into misreading a figure it otherwise transcribed correctly; and visual cues sometimes dominate over textual context (one example has a model fixated on a WWI-era image and picking "League of Nations" over the WWII-era "United Nations" the question actually asked about).

// does it hold up?

The eval read

MMMU-Pro is a well-targeted patch on a real, demonstrated leak — the 10.7-point drop from options alone, and the two worked examples in Figure 2, are hard evidence that MMMU's original number was inflated by guessable structure, not just conservative anecdote. But the fix imports its own assumptions, several of which the paper is admirably candid about in its own Limitations section.

The filter is calibrated to four specific 2024 open models, not to "text-solvability" in the abstract. A question survives if fewer than three of {Llama3-70B, Qwen2-72B, Yi-1.5-34B, Mixtral-8x22B} get it right in the majority of ten tries. That's a defensible proxy, but it's also a moving target: a stronger, more knowledgeable text-only LLM than any of these four (say, a 2026-era reasoning model) would very plausibly re-solve a nontrivial slice of the "filtered" 1,730 questions from priors alone, since the sieve was never adversarial against future models — only against 2024's open-weight text LLMs.

Human performance is approximated, not measured. The paper is transparent that it reuses MMMU's original human-eval numbers rather than re-running annotators on the new option sets and vision-only format, justified by an argument (unchanged question content, written-out reasoning in the original study) that is plausible but untested. A ceiling built this way could quietly misstate genuine human headroom on the harder 10-option and vision-only versions — an odd asymmetry for a benchmark whose entire premise is that surface changes to a question (more options, an embedded image) meaningfully change what's actually being measured.

Vision-only conflates two different skills. The Vision setting simultaneously tests OCR-style text extraction and multimodal reasoning over the original figure, now co-located in one photographed image. The OCR-prompt ablation (Table 2) is a good-faith attempt to disentangle these, and it does show OCR isn't the dominant bottleneck for strong models — but the manually photographed "backgrounds, font styles, font sizes" that create the vision-only images are themselves a new, uncontrolled nuisance variable (lighting, screenshot chrome, phone-camera artifacts) that MMMU-Pro doesn't ablate, so some of the vision-only drop for weaker models may be generic robustness-to-visual-noise rather than a genuine text+image integration failure.

baselines

Broad and current for late 2024 — GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, both Gemini 1.5 Pro releases, and a wide open-weight sweep (InternVL2, LLaVA family, VILA, MiniCPM, Idefics3, Phi-3.5-Vision). No o1-class reasoning model is included since none of the eligible ones existed yet at submission time.

contamination

Reduced but not eliminated relative to MMMU: 1,730 of the original questions survive the filter (just with more options and, for half, a re-photographed presentation), so anything already memorized about those specific stems from MMMU's own public release could still leak through, especially in the Standard-10 setting where the text is presented plainly again.

what's missed

No error-rate accounting for the human-expert approximation itself; no ablation of the vision-only pipeline's own photography artifacts (lighting/font/background) versus genuine text-image integration failure; the text-only filter is validated against only four specific models, not a moving standard.

would raise confidence

A fresh human study on the actual 10-option and vision-only item sets (even a modest-N one) to replace the approximation; a periodic re-filter against newer/stronger text-only models to keep the "no text shortcut" guarantee current; and a controlled ablation isolating photography noise from genuine cross-modal reasoning difficulty in the Vision setting.

// bottom line

MMMU-Pro correctly diagnosed and measured a real flaw in MMMU — guessable four-option structure plus text-only-solvable stems — and its three-step fix produces a genuinely harder, more discriminative benchmark: even GPT-4o's best score falls to roughly the low 50s. The construction is honest about its own approximations (human performance, the specific-model text filter), which is a point in its favor. But "robust" is relative to the text-only models used to build it and the human baseline reused from MMMU — not an absolute guarantee that a future model can't still find a different shortcut, textual or visual, that this particular pipeline wasn't built to catch.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. MMMU's biggest critique, addressed head-on. A three-step rebuild — filter out text-solvable questions using four open LLMs, widen 4 options to 10, and add a vision-only setting where the question is embedded in a photo — cuts scores across the board, with the option-widening step alone costing GPT-4o 10.7 points.
  2. The vision-only setting is the sharpest idea. Forcing models to read and see from a single image (rather than pre-separated text and image channels) exposes a genuine integration gap: strong OCR accuracy (>85% for top models) does not translate to strong Vision-setting accuracy, and reasoning errors (46%) — not perception or OCR errors — dominate the failure cases.
  3. Read "robust" carefully. The filter is validated against four specific 2024 open models, not against text-only solvability in the abstract, and the human baseline is approximated from MMMU's original study rather than freshly measured on the new format — both reasonable engineering calls, but both mean MMMU-Pro's numbers should anchor a ranking more confidently than they anchor an absolute ceiling.

Xiang Yue, Tianyu Zheng, Yuansheng Ni, Yubo Wang, Kai Zhang, Shengbang Tong, Yuxuan Sun, Botao Yu, Ge Zhang, Huan Sun, Yu Su, Wenhu Chen, Graham Neubig · 2024 · ACL 2025
